front: What are the three core principles of Object-Oriented Programming?
back: The three core principles are Encapsulation, Inheritance, and Polymorphism.

Encapsulation is the principle of bundling the data (variables) and the methods (functions) that operate on the data into a single unit, called a class, and restricting direct access to some of the object's components. In Java, this is achieved using `private` access modifiers and public getter/setter methods.

front: What is the main purpose of Inheritance?
back: Inheritance allows a new class (subclass/child class) to inherit the properties and methods of an existing class (superclass/parent class). It promotes code reusability and establishes a relationship between classes, forming a hierarchy.

front: Define Polymorphism and name a common way it is achieved in Java.
back: Polymorphism means 'many forms'. It is the ability of an object to take on many forms, typically allowing a single interface to be used for a general class of actions. The most common way it is achieved in Java is through method overriding, where a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in its superclass.

front: How do the three OOP principles work together?
back: They create robust and maintainable software. Encapsulation protects an object's internal state. Inheritance creates hierarchical relationships and promotes code reuse. Polymorphism allows those inherited methods to be used flexibly, with objects of different subclasses responding to the same method call in their own specific ways.
